course_info.title: Healthcare Industry Practicum
course_info.meeting_schedule: On-site at practicum organization + biweekly seminar

learning_objectives: Understand practicum expectations, learning objectives, and evaluation criteria
Establish professional conduct standards for the healthcare workplace
Set individual learning goals aligned with career objectives

lecture_notes.introduction: The practicum provides hands-on experience in a real healthcare informatics environment. You will apply classroom learning to actual organizational challenges while developing professional skills and industry connections.
key_concepts: Practicum structure: minimum 120 hours on-site over the semester
Site types: health systems, EHR vendors, consulting firms, public health agencies, startups
Learning agreement: jointly developed with site preceptor defining objectives and activities
Professional expectations: confidentiality, HIPAA compliance, workplace conduct
Reflective practice: connecting daily experiences to theoretical foundations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Panel discussion with prior practicum students sharing their experiences, challenges, and career impact

review.value: Formal document signed by student, site preceptor, and faculty advisor defining practicum objectives, activities, schedule, and evaluation criteria
On-site professional who supervises the student, assigns projects, provides feedback, and evaluates performance

learning_objectives: Analyze the informatics environment at the practicum site
Map organizational structure, IT systems, and governance processes
readings: Selected readings based on practicum site type

lecture_notes.introduction: Your first task at the practicum site is to understand the organization. This seminar discusses frameworks for organizational assessment and shares initial observations across the cohort.
key_concepts: Organizational assessment: mission, structure, IT landscape, strategic priorities
Informatics maturity assessment: where does the organization fall on the analytics maturity spectrum
Stakeholder mapping: who are the key informatics stakeholders and what are their priorities
Gap identification: where can your skills contribute most effectively
Professional networking: building relationships that will support your project and career
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents a 5-minute organizational overview of their practicum site, followed by group discussion of common themes

review.value: Assessment of where an organization falls on the spectrum from basic data collection to advanced analytics and AI-driven decision making
Building genuine professional relationships through curiosity, helpfulness, and follow-through - the practicum is a 15-week job interview

class_schedule.topic: Biweekly Seminar: Project Progress and Challenges
learning_objectives: Share practicum project progress with the seminar cohort
Problem-solve challenges using peer support and faculty guidance
Connect practicum experiences to health informatics theory

lecture_notes.introduction: This seminar provides a space to share progress, discuss challenges, and learn from each other's diverse practicum experiences across different organizational settings.
key_concepts: Progress reporting: what has been accomplished, what is in progress, what is planned
Challenge identification: technical, organizational, interpersonal barriers encountered
Peer problem-solving: leveraging diverse cohort experiences for creative solutions
Theory-practice connections: which classroom concepts are most relevant in practice
Professional development reflection: skills being developed and gaps identified
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured round-table discussion with each student sharing a 5-minute update and one challenge for group discussion

review.value: Identifying where classroom learning maps directly to practice and where real-world complexity creates challenges not covered in textbooks
Deliberately examining experiences to extract learning, connect to theory, and improve future performance

learning_objectives: Evaluate progress against learning agreement objectives at the midpoint
Incorporate preceptor feedback into remaining practicum activities
Adjust goals and timeline for the second half of the practicum

lecture_notes.introduction: The mid-point review is a formal checkpoint to assess progress, incorporate feedback, and ensure the practicum will meet its learning objectives by semester end.
key_concepts: Self-assessment: honest evaluation of progress against learning agreement
Preceptor feedback: incorporating on-site supervisor evaluation
Learning goal adjustment: narrowing or expanding based on mid-point progress
Time management: ensuring remaining hours are used effectively
Documentation quality: ensuring the reflective journal captures meaningful learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents their mid-point self-assessment and preceptor feedback summary, identifying adjustments for the remaining weeks

review.value: Structured reflection on what has been learned, what challenges remain, and what adjustments are needed for a successful practicum completion
Formal evaluation from the site supervisor covering professional skills, technical contribution, and areas for improvement

learning_objectives: Present practicum experience, project outcomes, and professional growth
Demonstrate integration of classroom learning with professional practice
Articulate career direction informed by practicum experience

lecture_notes.introduction: The final presentation synthesizes your practicum experience for the cohort, faculty, and invited site preceptors. This is an opportunity to showcase your professional development and contribution.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: organization context, project objectives, methodology, outcomes, lessons learned
Impact demonstration: quantifiable contributions to the organization
Professional growth narrative: how the practicum shaped your skills and career direction
Recommendations: what the organization should do next based on your work
Career implications: how the experience informs your job search and professional goals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student delivers a 15-minute final presentation followed by 10 minutes of Q&A from faculty and preceptors

review.value: Wherever possible, quantify your contribution: hours saved, errors reduced, users supported, reports produced, systems configured
Articulating how the practicum experience connects to your career goals demonstrates maturity and professional self-awareness

major_assignments.title: Practicum Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Complete a comprehensive practicum portfolio documenting your professional experience, project contributions, reflective learning, and career development throughout the practicum.
objectives: Document professional health informatics experience systematically
Demonstrate integration of classroom learning with professional practice
Produce tangible deliverables contributing to the practicum organization
Develop professional identity and career direction
deliverables: Learning agreement signed by student, preceptor, and faculty advisor (week 1)
Weekly reflective journal entries (15 entries minimum)
Final practicum report (15-20 pages) including project outcomes

criteria: Preceptor evaluation of professional skills and conduct
Reliability, initiative, and work quality
Effective communication with team members
Adherence to organizational policies including HIPAA
Meaningful contribution to the organization
Technical skills applied to real problems
Quality of deliverables produced for the organization
Impact documented and quantified where possible
Thoughtful journal entries connecting practice to theory
Honest self-assessment of strengths and growth areas
Evidence of professional growth over the semester
Constructive incorporation of feedback
Comprehensive final report with all required sections
Professional writing quality
Engaging and informative final presentation
Ability to answer questions about the experience
Strong professional performance evaluated by site preceptor
Meaningful project contribution to the organization
Deep reflective learning connecting theory and practice
Professional documentation and presentation

preceptor_evaluation.description: Site preceptor assessment of professional performance and contribution

project_deliverables.description: Quality and impact of work products produced during the practicum

reflective_journal.description: Weekly reflective entries demonstrating learning and professional growth

final_report.description: Comprehensive written documentation of the practicum experience

final_presentation.description: Professional presentation of practicum experience and outcomes

course_policies.attendance: On-site attendance per the learning agreement schedule is mandatory. Biweekly seminar sessions are mandatory. Absences from the practicum site must be communicated to both preceptor and faculty advisor.
course_policies.late_submissions: Reflective journal entries due weekly. Late entries lose 5% per day. Final report and presentation dates are non-negotiable.
course_policies.collaboration: Practicum work is individual. Seminar discussions are collaborative and support peer learning.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written reflections must be original. Work produced for the practicum organization remains the organization's intellectual property. Confidential information must never appear in academic submissions.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Varies by practicum site. Students must comply with the organization's technology policies and complete any required information security training.
